title: The Science of Sleep: Why We Dream

slides.heading: The Science of Sleep: Why We Dream
slides.body: Every night your brain embarks on a mysterious journey. Here's what science knows.

slides.heading: Sleep Is Not Passive Rest
slides.body: While your body rests, your brain cycles through distinct stages roughly every 90 minutes. Far from shutting down, it consolidates memories, flushes toxins, and regulates hormones — all critical to staying healthy and sharp.

slides.heading: The 4 Stages of Sleep
slides.body: Sleep falls into NREM (Stages 1–3) and REM phases. Light sleep (N1/N2) transitions you inward; deep slow-wave sleep (N3) restores the body; REM sleep activates the brain almost as if you're awake — and this is when dreams are most vivid.

slides.heading: What Actually Happens During REM
slides.body: In REM sleep, the brain's emotional and visual centers fire intensely while the prefrontal cortex — responsible for logic — goes quiet. Your muscles are temporarily paralyzed to prevent you from acting out your dreams.

slides.heading: Why Do We Dream?
slides.body: No single theory is settled, but leading ideas include memory consolidation (replaying experiences to encode them), emotional processing (working through stress and trauma), and threat simulation (rehearsing responses to danger in a safe environment).

slides.heading: Dreams & Memory
slides.body: Studies show that sleeping after learning a new skill dramatically improves retention. During REM, the hippocampus replays recent experiences and transfers them to long-term storage — essentially hitting 'save' on your day.

slides.heading: Lucid Dreaming: Taking the Wheel
slides.body: Lucid dreaming occurs when you become aware you're dreaming mid-dream. About 55% of people have experienced it at least once. Researchers at MIT have even demonstrated two-way communication with lucid dreamers using eye-movement signals.

slides.heading: Sleep Deprivation Is Serious
slides.body: Missing just one night of sleep spikes stress hormones and impairs judgment similarly to being drunk. Chronic sleep loss is linked to Alzheimer's, heart disease, and depression — in part because the brain can't clear toxic waste proteins without enough deep sleep.

slides.heading: Tips to Sleep (and Dream) Better
slides.body: Keep a consistent sleep schedule, avoid screens an hour before bed (blue light suppresses melatonin), keep your room cool and dark, and limit caffeine after 2 PM. A dream journal kept by your bed helps recall and explore your dreams.

slides.heading: Sleep Well, Live Better
slides.body: Sleep isn't lost time — it's your brain's most productive hours. Prioritize it like you would nutrition or exercise, and your waking life will thank you.
